Here is a timeline table tracing the evolution of Yemo, Yama, and their variants across cultures, integrating the development of Satan/Ahriman, and St. Christopher as a Yama-like figure. The table also includes major Yama/Yamantaka/Bhairava forms.

column4: Description / Evolutionary Note
Primordial twin in creation myth; killed by brother Manu, his body becomes the world.
Yemo splits into Yama (Vedic India) and Yima (Avestan Iran); both become first mortal and king of the dead.
God of death and justice, ruler of the underworld (Naraka), judge of souls.
Yima as first king; Ahriman (Angra Mainyu) as cosmic adversary, slayer of primordial beings, source of evil.
Yama as judge of the dead; Yamantaka (“Yama-slayer”) emerges as a wrathful deity who conquers Yama, symbolizing victory over death.
Yama becomes Yanluo Wang, ruler of the underworld in Chinese Buddhism and Daoist mythology.
Yama as Enma-ō, ruler of the afterlife, judge of souls.
Ahriman’s traits merge into Satan, the adversary in Christianity; both embody cosmic evil and rebellion.
Yamantaka as wrathful aspect of Manjushri, depicted with buffalo head, conquers Yama; many forms (blue/black/red, multi-headed).
Sometimes depicted with a dog or monstrous head (cynocephalus), linked in some legends to underworld guardianship and psychopomp roles, echoing Yama-like motifs.

column1: Yama/Yama Raja: Appears as the god of death and judge in Hinduism, Buddhism, and Jainism, often riding a buffalo and carrying a noose or mace811.
Yamantaka/Vajrabhairava: In Tibetan Buddhism, Yamantaka is the "destroyer of Yama," embodying the wrathful aspect of Manjushri. Major forms include:
Ahriman/Satan: Ahriman (Angra Mainyu) is the Zoroastrian principle of evil, later influencing the concept of Satan as the adversary in Abrahamic religions69.
St. Christopher: In some medieval legends, depicted as a cynocephalus (dog-headed giant), possibly echoing psychopomp or underworld guardian motifs similar to Yama or Anubis[*].
[*] The connection between St. Christopher and Yama is interpretative and based on the motif of monstrous or animal-headed guardians of the threshold to the afterlife, found in multiple mythologies.

This table illustrates the transformation of the primordial Indo-European Yemo into various death, afterlife, and adversary figures across Eurasian religions, highlighting the persistent motif of the judge or conqueror of death, and the integration of dualistic evil (Ahriman/Satan) into later religious frameworks. Yamantaka’s Buddhist forms represent the transcendence and conquest of death, while St. Christopher’s monstrous depictions echo the ancient threshold guardian archetype248107691.

column1: Yemo/Yama Lineage: The primordial Indo-European myth of Yemo splits into Yama (Vedic India) and Yima (Iran), both rulers of the dead. In Buddhist and Tantric traditions, Yama evolves into Yamantaka, who conquers death.

Satan/Ahriman/Devil Development: The concept of a cosmic adversary emerges in Zoroastrianism (Ahriman) and is later adopted and transformed in Jewish and Christian traditions. In early Israelite religion, there is no devil figure; Satan emerges as a member of the divine court during the Second Temple period, only later becoming the personification of evil in Christianity123578.

St. Christopher as Yama-like Figure: Medieval legends sometimes depict St. Christopher as monstrous or dog-headed, resonating with the archetype of underworld guardians such as Yama, Anubis, and related figures6.

Yama/Yamantaka Variants: In Buddhist and Tantric iconography, Yama is the judge of the dead, while Yamantaka (Vajrabhairava) is the wrathful conqueror of Yama, depicted in multiple forms (e.g., Krishna Yamari, Rakta Yamari).

column1: The role of chthonic deities and underworld rulers-especially as judges of the dead-shows strong continuity across Indo-European cultures, all tracing back to PIE motifs of earth, death, and the afterlife. While Yama, Hades, Veles, and others share deep mythological and sometimes etymological roots, there is no historical or linguistic connection between these figures and YHVH/Yahweh/Jehovah, whose origins are Semitic and unrelated to the Indo-European tradition. The devil/adversary motif (Ahriman, Satan) is a later development, often influenced by dualistic cosmologies but not directly tied to the PIE chthonic tradition.

column4: Prophetic and royal opposition to "idolatry"; Asherah worship in temples; Elijah vs. prophets of Baal and Asherah
Moabite Stone (Mesha Stele) contains earliest known extra-biblical reference to Yahweh

column3: Moves toward monolatry/monotheism, but popular religion includes Asherah
column4: Assyrian conquest of Israel (722 BCE); reforms in Judah begin

column3: Josiah’s reforms: centralization of Yahweh worship, purging of Asherah and Baal
column4: Josiah removes Asherah from Temple, burns symbols, suppresses goddess worship

column3: Monotheism solidifies among elite; popular religion still varied
column4: Babylonian conquest; Temple destroyed; exile accelerates monotheistic Yahwism
Return from exile; Second Temple built; official suppression of earlier syncretism

column3: Yahweh becomes sole deity; Judaism revised and canonized
column4: Yahweh as state god of Judah; monotheism established; avoidance of pronouncing the name

column3: Name YHWH (Tetragrammaton) not pronounced; "Adonai" or "Elohim" substituted
column4: Greek and Latin translations use "Kyrios" and "Dominus"; "the LORD" in English Bibles

column3: Vowel points of "Adonai" or "Elohim" added to YHWH, creating "Yehovah"/"Jehovah" in later Christian use
column4: Jews avoid pronouncing YHWH; Christian scholars Latinize the name as "Jehovah"

column1: Origins: Yahweh may have developed from a Canaanite deity, possibly associated with metallurgy, and the name appears to predate Moses125.
Earliest Attestation: The Mesha Stele (Moabite Stone, c. 850 BCE) is the earliest known non-biblical inscription mentioning Yahweh49.
Biblical Usage: The name YHWH is used throughout the Hebrew Bible, with its meaning traditionally linked to the Hebrew verb "to be" (Exodus 3:14)6789.

Pronunciation: The scholarly consensus is that the original pronunciation was "Yahweh," though the exact vowels are uncertain. Jews eventually ceased to pronounce the name, substituting "Adonai" (Lord) or "Elohim" (God) in reading34789.
Later Developments: The Latinized form "Jehovah" arose in the Middle Ages when Christian scholars combined the consonants YHWH with the vowels of "Adonai"34789.

This timeline integrates the development of Yahweh’s name, its earliest attestations, and its evolving religious significance alongside the broader evolution of Israelite and Jewish religion.
